interactions.summary: Jiang completes the hell/purgatory distinction and introduces Statius as a soul whose joy marks successful penance and imminent access to heaven.
Statius identifies himself as an epic poet inspired by Virgil, loving Virgil so much he would remain in Purgatory another year to meet him; Jiang uses this to expose Virgil's false baptism rule.
Jiang moves to Beatrice's descent and sets up Virgil's final test: Beatrice could help him leave limbo, while Dante longs to share his joy with Virgil, but Virgil disappears.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang interpreting the final Virgil-Beatrice scene.

speaker_notes.note: These refs contain extended quoted or paraphrased lines from Dante alongside Jiang's interpretation.

claims.claim: Hell and purgatory contain sinners of similar gravity, but purgatory differs because its souls admit sin and commit to redeeming themselves.
claims.temporal_scope: Lecture model of Dante's moral geography.

claims.claim: Statius's release is marked by an earthquake because the mountain celebrates when a soul has cleansed itself enough to enter heaven.
Statius is an epic poet like Virgil and treats Virgil as holy fire, mother, and nurse of his own poetry.
Statius proves Virgil was lying or wrong because Statius, another unbaptized Roman epic poet, can ascend to heaven while Virgil cannot.
Statius can go to heaven because he wants to improve and admit flaws in his understanding; Virgil cannot because he refuses that introspection.
claims.temporal_scope: Lecture explanation of Statius versus Virgil.

claims.claim: At the moment Dante sees Beatrice, he wants to share his joy with Virgil as a father figure, but Virgil has run away at the climax of his completed quest.

signature_moments.moment: Hell is where you go when you refuse to admit you are wrong.
signature_moments.source_phrase: If you refuse to admit you're wrong
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It turns damnation from legal punishment into a psychology of stubbornness.

signature_moments.moment: Statius calls Virgil the mother and holy fire of his poetry.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Virgil is the God, the mother, who nursed me
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It intensifies the irony: Virgil can inspire the person who rises while remaining trapped himself.

signature_moments.moment: At the instant Dante wants to share Beatrice with his father-guide, Virgil has run away.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is the emotional climax of Jiang's trusted-guide-becomes-obstacle reading.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Roman epic poet in Purgatory whose ascent exposes Virgil's false claim that unbaptized merit cannot rise.
The willingness to admit fault, do penance, and revise one's understanding.
